questions.question: Explain the three basic label operations performed by an LSR with examples.
keyPoints: PUSH: Adding label at ingress (e.g., adding label 100 to IP packet)
SWAP: Replacing label in transit (e.g., swapping label 100 with 200)
POP: Removing label at egress (e.g., removing label to deliver IP packet)

questions.question: Differentiate between Control Plane and Data Plane functions in an LSR.
keyPoints: Control Plane: Handles routing, label distribution (LDP), FEC assignment, maintains LIB
Data Plane: Performs actual packet forwarding using LFIB, label operations (push/swap/pop)

questions.question: What is Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)? Explain its advantages.
keyPoints: PHP is when penultimate router removes label before sending to egress
Reduces processing burden on egress router
Improves packet forwarding efficiency
Saves one label lookup operation at egress

questions.question: Describe the structure of the MPLS shim header with field sizes.
keyPoints: 32-bit header inserted between IP header and data link layer
20-bit label field (up to 2^20 labels)
3-bit EXP field for QoS
1-bit BoS (Bottom of Stack) flag
8-bit TTL for loop prevention

questions.question: Explain the difference between LIB and LFIB in LSR operation.
keyPoints: LIB (Label Information Base): Control plane table with all learned label bindings
LFIB (Label Forwarding Information Base): Data plane table with active forwarding entries
LIB may contain multiple labels per FEC, LFIB selects best path
LFIB used for actual packet forwarding

questions.question: What is Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC) in MPLS? On what basis are packets classified into FECs?
keyPoints: FEC is a group of packets forwarded identically through MPLS network
Classification criteria: Destination IP prefix, QoS requirements, VPN membership
Ingress LSR assigns packet to FEC at entry point
All packets in same FEC get same label treatment

questions.question: Explain the complete process of packet forwarding in an MPLS network from ingress to egress, detailing the role of each LSR type.
keyPoints: Ingress LSR (LER): Classifies packet into FEC, pushes label (label imposition)
Core LSRs: Swap incoming label with outgoing label based on LFIB
Penultimate Hop: Optionally performs PHP (popping label)
Egress LSR: Removes final label (POP), delivers IP packet to destination
Role of LFIB in forwarding decisions
Label distribution via LDP between LSRs

questions.question: Compare Edge LSR and Core LSR in terms of their functions, operations, and location in the MPLS domain.
keyPoints: Edge LSR: Located at domain boundaries, performs label imposition/removal
Core LSR: Located in network core, performs label swapping only
Edge LSR classifies packets into FECs, Core LSR uses label lookup
Edge LSR connects to traditional IP networks
Core LSR focuses on fast forwarding, simpler operations
Both maintain LIB and LFIB for operation

questions.question: Explain Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) and its messages. How does LDP establish LSPs in an MPLS network?
keyPoints: LDP is the protocol for distributing label bindings between LSRs
LDP Messages: Discovery (Hello), Session (Keepalive), Advertisement (Mapping), Notification
LDP adjacency formation between neighboring LSRs
Label request and mapping messages for FEC-to-label binding
LSP establishment through label distribution path
Difference between LDP and RSVP-TE

questions.question: Describe the well-known label values in MPLS and their specific purposes.
keyPoints: Label 0: IPv4 Explicit Null - preserves QoS info to egress
Label 1: Router Alert - alerts routers to process packet
Label 2: IPv6 Explicit Null - similar to label 0 for IPv6
Label 3: Implicit Null - used for PHP (penultimate hop popping)
Labels 4-15: Reserved for future use
Labels 16-1023: Reserved for LDP/RSVP-TE
Labels 1024 and above: Available for use
